topics.name: AI & Multi-agent Systems
topics.description: Game theory provides a natural framework for understanding and designing AI agents that must coordinate, compete, or cooperate with others. We use evolutionary game theory and reinforcement learning to study how cooperation can emerge in decentralised multi-agent systems.

topics.name: Social Dynamics
topics.description: Human societies face collective action problems everywhere. We use game theory and simulations to understand how cooperation and social norms emerge and break down.

topics.name: Game Theory
topics.description: We work on foundational questions in evolutionary game theory — how strategies evolve in repeated interactions, what role can learning play in equilibrium selection, and how to use computation and AI to answer questions relevant to strategic interaction and decision making.

topics.name: Science of Science
topics.description: Scientific institutions — journals, peer review, public communication — are themselves strategic systems. We apply game theory to understand how incentives shape the quality of science and its public understanding.

papers.title: Group Selection as a Safeguard Against AI Substitution
papers.authors: Qiankun Zhong, Thomas F. Eisenmann, <u>Julian García</u> and Iyad Rahwan
papers.summary: Dependence on generative AI can diminish cultural variance and diversity, especially in creative work. We use evolutionary theory to examine how different AI usage patterns — tool use vs full substitution — affect collective outcomes, and show that group selection can safeguard against homogenisation.

papers.title: Hybrid Human-Agent Social Dilemmas in Energy Markets
papers.authors: Isuri Perera, Frits de Nijs and <u>Julian García</u>
papers.summary: What happens when humans and autonomous agents interact strategically in energy markets? We show that artificial agents using observable signals can improve coordination, and that early adopters face no structural disadvantage despite potential free-rider dynamics.

papers.title: Learning to cooperate against ensembles of diverse opponents
papers.authors: Isuri Perera, Frits de Nijs and <u>Julian García</u>
papers.summary: How can an RL agent learn to cooperate when facing many different, anonymous opponents? Inspired by evolutionary game theory, we design agents that maintain cooperation across diverse partners without needing to model each one individually.

papers.title: Cooperation and Reputation Dynamics with Reinforcement Learning
papers.authors: Nicolas Anastassacos, <u>Julian García</u>, Stephen Hailes and Mirco Musolesi
papers.summary: Can RL agents independently learn to use reputation to sustain cooperation? We show they can — but only with the right nudges. A combination of seeding and intrinsic rewards based on altruistic reciprocation stabilises cooperation even in fully decentralised settings.

papers.title: Cooperative Dilemmas in Rational Debate
papers.authors: Toby Handfield, <u>Julian García</u>, Christian Hilbe and Shang Long Yeo
papers.summary: Rational debate requires cooperation — everyone benefits from reaching true beliefs, but nobody wants to be the one to change their mind. We show that this tension creates social dilemmas where individually optimal strategies undermine collective truth-seeking.

papers.title: Evolution of coordinated punishment to enforce cooperation from an unbiased strategy space
papers.venue: Journal of the Royal Society Interface
papers.summary: Most models of punishment restrict which strategies are available. We include all possible strategies — including anti-social ones — and find that coordinated pro-social institutions can still emerge, but only when individuals can observe and condition on whether such institutions exist.

papers.title: Direct reciprocity in structured populations
papers.authors: Matthijs van Veelen, <u>Julian García</u>, David G. Rand and Martin A. Nowak
papers.summary: Direct reciprocity alone does not lead to high cooperation in well-mixed populations. But combine repeated interactions with even a small amount of population structure, and conditional cooperation thrives.

papers.title: Picking strategies in games of cooperation
papers.summary: How modellers choose which strategies to include in evolutionary game theory models can quietly shape the conclusions. We propose principles for more systematic strategy selection and argue that AI methods can help build richer models of cooperation.

papers.title: Repeated games with partner choice
papers.authors: Christopher Graser, Takako Fujiwara-Greve, <u>Julian García</u> and Matthijs van Veelen
papers.summary: What happens when players can walk away and find a new partner? The option to leave actually increases cooperation — people end up choosing dependable partners, creating a form of assortment that standard repeated game models miss.

papers.title: No strategy can win in the repeated prisoner's dilemma
papers.summary: For every Nash equilibrium in the repeated prisoner's dilemma, there are sequences of mutants that destabilise it. Populations cycle between equilibria with different levels of cooperation — and this instability is inescapable regardless of how strategies are represented.

papers.title: Academic journals, incentives, and the quality of peer review: a model
papers.authors: Kevin J. S. Zollman, <u>Julian García</u> and Toby Handfield
papers.summary: We model how different incentives shape journal behaviour in peer review. When journals chase high rejection rates rather than quality, they are incentivised to make review less accurate — leading to worse papers being published.

papers.title: A technocognitive approach to detecting fallacies in climate misinformation
papers.authors: Francisco Zanartu, John Cook, Markus Wagner and <u>Julian García</u>
papers.summary: We train models to detect logical fallacies in climate misinformation, achieving 2.5–3.5x better detection than prior work. Fake experts and anecdotal arguments are easiest to catch; fallacies requiring background knowledge remain harder.
